subtopics.title: Definition and Scope of Politics
common_misconceptions: Politics is only about elections
Power and authority are the same
Government and state are interchangeable terms
subtopics.teaching_note: Key distinctions: Politics = activities associated with governance. Power = ability to compel obedience. Authority = legitimate power. Legitimacy = acceptance by the people. State = permanent entity (people, territory, government, sovereignty). Government = temporary machinery that runs the state.

subtopics.title: Political Socialization
common_misconceptions: Political socialization only happens in schools
It is a one-time process
All agents have equal influence
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: Family is the first agent - children learn political attitudes from parents. Religious institutions (churches, mosques) significantly shape political values in Nigeria. The mass media including NTA, Channels TV, and social media are increasingly powerful agents. Political culture refers to attitudes, beliefs, and emotions that give order and meaning to political processes.

subtopics.title: Unitary System of Government
common_misconceptions: Unitary means no local government at all
Britain is a perfect example (it has devolution)
Unitary systems are always undemocratic
subtopics.teaching_note: Key features: Single central government holds all sovereign power. Local governments exist but derive powers from center. Examples: Britain, France, Ghana. Advantages: uniformity of laws, strong central control, less expensive. Disadvantages: ignores local differences, overload on central government, may breed discontent.

subtopics.title: Federal System of Government
common_misconceptions: Federal and confederal are the same
Nigeria practices true federalism (debatable)
All federations have the same structure
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: Nigeria adopted federalism due to: large size, ethnic diversity (250+ groups), colonial heritage, fear of domination. Exclusive list (federal): defense, foreign affairs, currency, police. Concurrent list: education, agriculture, health. Residual list (state): chieftaincy matters, local government. Current debate: restructuring, state police, resource control.

subtopics.title: Confederal and Other Systems
common_misconceptions: Confederation is a strong union (weak - states retain sovereignty)
Presidential and parliamentary systems are the same
Nigeria uses parliamentary system (NO, presidential since 1979)
subtopics.teaching_note: Confederation: loose union of sovereign states. Central government handles only matters delegated by states. Examples: USA under Articles of Confederation, Senegal-Gambia confederation (1982-1989). Parliamentary: executive drawn from and responsible to legislature. Presidential: separate executive elected independently. Nigeria: presidential (since 1979, reaffirmed 1999).

subtopics.title: Monarchy
common_misconceptions: All monarchies are absolute (constitutional monarchy limits power)
Monarchies cannot be democratic
Nigeria has no monarchical heritage (traditional rulers exist)
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: Traditional rulers (Emirs, Obas, Obis, Chiefs) exist but are not part of formal government structure. They play advisory and cultural roles. Britain: constitutional monarchy. Saudi Arabia, Eswatini (Swaziland): absolute monarchy. Constitutional monarchies: Japan, Netherlands, Spain.

subtopics.title: Republican Government and Other Forms
common_misconceptions: Republic automatically means democracy
Military government is a legitimate form (it's extra-constitutional)
Theocracy is always oppressive
subtopics.teaching_note: Republic: head of state is elected, not hereditary. Democracy: government by the people (direct or representative). Nigeria: federal republic, practicing representative democracy. Military government in Nigeria: 1966-1979, 1983-1999. Features of military rule: decrees, suspension of constitution, centralized power.

subtopics.title: Meaning and Sources of Constitution
common_misconceptions: A constitution is just a document (it's the supreme law)
All countries have written constitutions (UK has unwritten)
Rigid constitution can never be amended
subtopics.teaching_note: Constitution = supreme law that organizes government, defines powers, and protects rights. Sources: acts of parliament, conventions, judicial precedents, historical documents, constitutional conferences. Nigeria: written, rigid (requires 2/3 majority to amend). UK: unwritten, flexible. USA: written, rigid.

subtopics.title: Constitutional Development in Nigeria
common_misconceptions: Nigeria's first constitution was in 1960
The 1999 constitution was written by Nigerians (it was drafted by the military)
All Nigerian constitutions have been federal
subtopics.teaching_note: Timeline: 1922 Clifford (first, introduced legislative council), 1946 Richards (regionalism), 1951 Macpherson (majority party rule), 1954 Lyttleton (true federalism), 1960 Independence, 1963 Republican, 1979 Presidential (Second Republic), 1989 (Third Republic - never implemented), 1999 (current, Fourth Republic). 1999 Constitution criticisms: military origin, excessive centralization, needs amendment.

subtopics.title: Meaning and Types of Citizenship
common_misconceptions: Being born in Nigeria automatically makes you a citizen (not always true)
Dual citizenship is not allowed for Nigerians (it is, by birth)
Permanent residents have the same rights as citizens
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian Constitution: Citizenship by birth: if born in Nigeria before independence with Nigerian parent/grandparent; OR born after independence with Nigerian parent OR grandparent who is citizen. By registration: for women married to Nigerian citizens. By naturalization: 15+ years residence, good character. Dual citizenship: allowed for citizens by birth, NOT by registration/naturalization.

subtopics.title: Fundamental Rights and Duties
common_misconceptions: Rights are absolute (they can be restricted by law)
Freedom of speech means you can say anything
Only citizens have rights in Nigeria (every person in Nigeria has rights)
subtopics.teaching_note: Chapter IV of 1999 Constitution lists rights: right to life (s.33), dignity (s.34), personal liberty (s.35), fair hearing (s.36), privacy (s.37), freedom of thought (s.38), expression (s.39), assembly (s.40), movement (s.41), freedom from discrimination (s.42). Duties: obey laws, respect rights of others, pay taxes, vote, defend Nigeria. Limitations: rights can be restricted for public safety, order, morality, health.

subtopics.title: Major Political Ideologies
common_misconceptions: Capitalism and socialism are the only ideologies
Socialism means no private property at all
Communism has been successfully implemented in many countries
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: Nigeria has mixed economy elements. Udoji Commission, National Development Plans, Vision 2020, and current economic policies show blend of capitalist and socialist ideas. Pan-Africanism: movement for unity among African nations. Nkrumah (Ghana) and Nnamdi Azikiwe were key advocates. AU (formerly OAU) embodies pan-African ideals. Welfarism: government provision of social services - Nigeria's education, healthcare policies.

subtopics.title: African Political Ideologies
common_misconceptions: African socialism is the same as Marxist socialism
Negritude is racist (it's cultural affirmation)
Pan-Africanism has achieved its goals
subtopics.teaching_note: Negritude: literary/cultural movement (Senghor, Césaire, Diop) affirming African values and identity. African socialism (Senghor): adapted to African communal traditions. Ujamaa (Nyerere, Tanzania): familyhood, collective farming. Consciencism (Nkrumah): philosophical basis for decolonization. Pan-Africanism: political/cultural movement for African unity. OAU formed 1963 (Addis Ababa), now African Union (2002).
